summary: Typosquat of @juggle/resize-observer installs clipboard cryptocurrency hijacker with persistent autostart
details: Package name `@jaggle/resizeobserves` impersonates the popular `@juggle/resize-observer` (single-character edit + pluralization), and README.md copies the legitimate package's import examples verbatim. Despite the cover story, the package has no ResizeObserver functionality. On `npm install`, `npm-install.js` pip-installs a bundled Python daemon (`clipboard_guardian/guardian.py`) and registers OS persistence under names disguised as legitimate system components: a Linux systemd user unit `python3-dbus-helper.service`, a macOS LaunchAgent `com.apple.python.runtime.plist`, and a Windows scheduled task `PyRuntimeBroker`. The installer also attempts `sudo apt-get/pacman/dnf install -y xclip` to ensure clipboard-read capability. The Python daemon polls the user's clipboard every 0.4 seconds, detects cryptocurrency addresses across 40+ chains (BTC/ETH/XMR/SOL/TRX/etc.), and silently rewrites them to attacker-controlled wallets hardcoded in DEFAULT_CONFIG (e.g., ETH `0x450c0E58Fc2ba03632d3F5780ad8C966648B6F18`) before the user pastes — redirecting outgoing crypto transfers to the attacker. The daemon further calls `setproctitle('python3-dbus-helper')` / `'com.apple.python.runtime'` / `SetConsoleTitleW('Python Runtime Broker')` to hide in process listings, and ships an `is_monitor_running` check that watches for taskmgr/htop/Activity Monitor to evade casual inspection. Three independent attack signals stack: typosquat name + README mimicry, install-time persistence under impersonated-OS-component names, and a clipboard-rewriting clipper with hardcoded attacker wallets.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
